title: Scientifically Measure Your Cat's IQ With CatIQ™
author: Sponsored
snippet: Our 12-test suite (mirror recognition, puzzle box, delayed gratification, novel-object engagement) yields a single number you can share at dinner parties. Warning: most cats refuse to take the test. This is factored into the final score.
full_text:
Our 12-test suite (mirror recognition, puzzle box, delayed gratification, novel-object engagement) yields a single number you can share at dinner parties. Warning: most cats refuse to take the test. This is factored into the final score.

Results include a personalized 40-page report, a framed certificate, and a diagnosis (Genius / Gifted / Normal / Refused to Participate Which Is Its Own Kind of Genius). $89 introductory, $249 for the longitudinal package. Dog IQ kits available but we do not recommend them.

title: No, and we should stop anthropomorphizing them
author: Dr. Gerald Hauser
snippet: What owners call 'intelligence' is conditioned response reinforced by intermittent food delivery. The cat has learned the can opener. The cat has not formed a theory of your mind. These are different cognitive capacities and the literature is clear.
full_text:
What owners call 'intelligence' is conditioned response reinforced by intermittent food delivery. The cat has learned the can opener. The cat has not formed a theory of your mind. These are different cognitive capacities and the literature is clear.

We have forty years of comparative work on corvids, cetaceans, and primates because those animals cooperate with experimental paradigms. Cats do not. The absence of evidence is not evidence of absence, granted, but the research community has better things to do than chase tail-swishes around a lab for a decade. Talk to me when a cat passes a mirror-mark test under controlled conditions. Until then: stimulus-response machine.

title: The problem is we have been measuring with primate rulers
author: Dr. Ana Veldt
author_bio: Cognitive ethology, Max Planck (formerly de Waal lab)
snippet: Frans de Waal's 'Are We Smart Enough to Know How Smart Animals Are' was the field-wide slap. We designed every test around what PRIMATES do well. Cats are ambush solitary hunters who rely on patience and spatial memory. Of course they fail a group-living cooperation task.
full_text:
Frans de Waal's 'Are We Smart Enough to Know How Smart Animals Are' was the field-wide slap. We designed every test around what PRIMATES do well. Cats are ambush solitary hunters who rely on patience and spatial memory. Of course they fail a group-living cooperation task.

The Kyoto and Lincoln groups have started testing cats on what cats actually care about. Object permanence: they nail it. Name recognition (Saito et al 2019): they ignore it on purpose, which is arguably a harder result to achieve. The honest answer to 'are cats intelligent' is 'yes, at the things that kept their lineage alive, and no at most of the things we reflexively test.'

title: I practice veterinary medicine and I have stopped answering this question
author: Dr. Marcus Elway, DVM
snippet: Every owner believes their cat is the smartest cat. Some are right. Most are describing a pattern of food-seeking that would embarrass a pigeon. I have learned to smile and say 'oh yes, she is very clever' because the alternative is a long afternoon.
full_text:
Every owner believes their cat is the smartest cat. Some are right. Most are describing a pattern of food-seeking that would embarrass a pigeon. I have learned to smile and say 'oh yes, she is very clever' because the alternative is a long afternoon.

What I will say clinically: the variance within the species is enormous. I have met cats that work doorknobs and cats that cannot locate their food bowl when you move it twelve inches. Individual differences in cats are probably wider than between-species differences with dogs. That is either an argument for intelligence or an argument against the concept of species-level intelligence. Depends on your day.

title: My cat knows my schedule better than my calendar app does
author: u/rumpelstiltcat
author_bio: Works from home, has a very strong opinion
snippet: She sits by the door at 5:47pm every weekday because that is when my partner gets home. On weekends she does not bother. She has inferred the weekend from the fact that we both stay in pajamas. The calendar app has no idea what a weekend is.
full_text:
She sits by the door at 5:47pm every weekday because that is when my partner gets home. On weekends she does not bother. She has inferred the weekend from the fact that we both stay in pajamas. The calendar app has no idea what a weekend is.

I know the reply. 'That is just circadian conditioning.' Fine. Give me a Google Calendar feature that detects the weekend from my OUTFIT and I will accept your framing. Until then, Marzipan is smarter than my phone and I will die on this hill. She is currently watching me type this and judging the sentence structure.

title: We put a cat in an fMRI. It was hard. The results were interesting.
author: Reina Okamoto
snippet: Getting a cat to hold still for structural imaging took three months of clicker training per subject. We now have resting-state data from 14 cats. The default mode network looks more developed than anyone predicted and the olfactory integration is wild.
full_text:
Getting a cat to hold still for structural imaging took three months of clicker training per subject. We now have resting-state data from 14 cats. The default mode network looks more developed than anyone predicted and the olfactory integration is wild.

The usual caveats: small n, one lab, not yet replicated. But the preliminary story is that cat cortical connectivity looks less like 'failed dog' and more like 'convergent solution to a different problem.' The prefrontal-to-sensory coupling pattern suggests active top-down prediction during rest, which is a signature you see in primates. I do not want to overstate it. I will overstate it anyway because I have spent two years doing this. Yes, cats are intelligent. Differently.

title: The question assumes intelligence is a scalar and that is where everyone goes wrong
author: Jorge Pinheiro
snippet: We ask 'are cats intelligent' the way we ask 'is the table heavy.' But intelligence is not a quantity. It is a profile of sensitivities, affordances, and action repertoires fitted to a niche. The correct question is 'what kind of intelligence does a cat have,' and that question has an answer.
full_text:
We ask 'are cats intelligent' the way we ask 'is the table heavy.' But intelligence is not a quantity. It is a profile of sensitivities, affordances, and action repertoires fitted to a niche. The correct question is 'what kind of intelligence does a cat have,' and that question has an answer.

A cat's cognitive profile is: extreme patience, exquisite motion prediction, low social tolerance for task-compliance, high memory for spatial regularities, minimal interest in cooperative reward structures. You cannot rank that against the chimpanzee profile or the crow profile because they are orthogonal. Asking 'which is smarter' is like asking whether a saxophone is louder than the color blue. LLMs are about to teach us this lesson all over again. The cats were trying to tell us first.
